examples: 저와 함께 걸을 준비 되셨나요? = Are you ready to walk with me?
매일 걸어요. = I walk every day.
notes:
걷다 : ㄷ-irregular — stem 걷 changes to 걸 before vowel endings
걷다 → 걸어요 (not 걷어요) | 걸을 (future modifier) | 걸어서 (by walking)
Contrast: 달리다 (to run), 뛰다 (to run/jump)
trap: ㄷ irregular: 걷 → 걸 before vowels (걸어요, 걸으면, 걸을)

english: (verb) to become / to be done / can / to work out
examples: 준비 되셨나요? = Are you ready? (lit. has it become ready?)
안 돼요. = That won't do / I can't.
notes:
되다 : one of the most versatile verbs
준비가 되다 : to be ready | 안 되다 : to not work / not be allowed
Passive: 바뀌다 = 바꾸다+이다; but 되다 also forms passives: 먹히다 vs 됩니다
~게 되다 : come to (gradually): 알게 됐어요 = I came to know
trap: 돼요 = 되어요 (contracted). 되요 is a common misspelling.

english: (verb) to see / to look / to watch / to try
examples: 세상을 보는 눈이 넓어졌어요. = My view of the world widened.
한번 해 봐요. = Try doing it once.
notes:
보다 : core meanings — to see (눈으로 보다), to watch (영화 보다), to look at
~아/어 보다 : try doing (경험해 봐요 = try experiencing it)
~게 되다 vs ~아/어 보다: 보게 됐어요 (came to see) vs 봐요 (try seeing)
보다 also means 'than' as a particle: 나보다 커요 = taller than me

english: (verb) to live / to reside / to be alive
examples: 매일 똑같은 곳에서 똑같은 삶을 살다 보면… = When you live the same life in the same place day after day…
어디 살아요? = Where do you live?
notes:
살다 : ㄹ-irregular — ㄹ drops before ㄴ, ㅂ, 시, (으)세요
살아요 (live) | 사는 (who lives) | 사람 | 삶 (noun: life)
살다 보면 : 'if you keep living (like that)' — ongoing habitual condition

examples: 평소에는 몰랐던 내 모습을 알게 돼요. = You come to know sides of yourself you didn't know.
알아요. = I know / I understand.
notes:
알다 : ㄹ-irregular — ㄹ drops before ㄴ, ㅂ, 시
알아요 | 아는 (who knows) | 알면 (if you know) | 아세요? (do you know? honorific)
Negative: 모르다 (not to know) — separate verb, not 알지 않다
trap: 알아요 NOT 알어요. 모르다 is the irregular negative, not 안 알다.

english: (verb) to exist / to have / to be (at a location)
examples: 한국에 있어요. = I am in Korea.
시간이 있어요? = Do you have time?
notes:
있다 : existence (거기 있어요 = it's there) OR possession (돈이 있어요 = I have money)
Opposite: 없다 (to not exist / to not have)
있어요 → 있어 (casual) | 계세요 (honorific form of 있다 for people)

english: (verb) to not exist / to not have / to be absent
examples: 시간이 없어요. = I don't have time.
여유가 없는 삶 = a life with no breathing room
notes:
없다 : opposite of 있다
없어요 → 없어 (casual) | 안 계세요 (honorific: is not here)
아무도 없다 = there is nobody | 돈이 없다 = to not have money

examples: 여행을 가요. = I go on a trip.
학교에 가요. = I go to school.
notes:
가다 : direction away from speaker (opposite: 오다 = to come)
가요 | 가서 (go and then) | 가면 (if you go) | 가는 것 (the act of going)
다녀오다 : go and come back (round trip)

examples: 한국에서 왔어요. = I came from Korea.
내일 와요. = Come tomorrow.
notes:
오다 : movement toward speaker or toward a reference point
왔어요 (came) | 와서 (coming and then) | 오면 (if you come) | 오는 것
들어오다 (come in) | 나오다 (come out) | 돌아오다 (come back)

examples: 여행은 우리에게 선물을 줘요. = Travel gives us a gift.
저한테 주세요. = Please give it to me.
notes:
주다 : to give to someone else
줘요 = 주어요 (contracted) | 드리다 = humble form (give to someone senior)
~아/어 주다 : do something FOR someone (도와줘요 = help me [for my benefit])
trap: 줘요 NOT 주어요 in speech. 드리다 when giving to elders/seniors.

examples: 밥을 먹고 자요. = I eat and then sleep.
마음이 설레고 기분이 좋아요. = My heart flutters and I feel good.
notes:
고 : attaches to verb/adjective stem; connects two clauses
먹고, 걷고, 좋고 — same subject usually shared
Opposite order matters: A고 B = A first then B

english: (ending) because / so / and then — causal or sequential (bright vowel stem)
examples: 좋아서 웃어요. = I'm laughing because I like it.
일어나서 씻어요. = I get up and then wash.
notes:
아서/어서 : causal (reason) or sequential (first A then B)
아서 after ㅏ or ㅗ stem | 어서 after all others | 해서 from 하다 verbs
가서 (← 가다) | 좋아서 (← 좋다) | 먹어서 (← 먹다)
Unlike 고, cannot be used with imperative or propositive

english: (ending) because / so / and then — causal or sequential (dark vowel stem)
examples: 피곤해서 일찍 잤어요. = I went to sleep early because I was tired.
먹어서 배불러요. = I ate, so I'm full.
notes:
아서/어서 : same meaning, different vowel harmony form
어서 after stems without ㅏ or ㅗ | 아서 after ㅏ/ㅗ stems | 해서 from 하다
해서 ← 하다: 공부해서 (because I studied)

english: (ending) if / when — conditional (after vowel or ㄹ stem)
examples: 여행을 가면 행복해요. = If/when I travel, I'm happy.
알면 알수록 좋아요. = The more you know, the better.
notes:
면/으면 : conditional — 'if' for hypothetical, 'when' for habitual
면 after vowel or ㄹ stem | 으면 after other consonants
가면, 살면, 알면 | 먹으면, 좋으면

examples: 비가 오으면 → 비가 오면. = If it rains. (오다+면, vowel, so 면)
먹으면 배불러요. = If you eat, you'll be full.
notes:
면/으면 : conditional
으면 after consonant stems (먹으면, 없으면, 좋으면)
면 after vowel or ㄹ stems (가면, 살면, 알면)

examples: 여행이 좋지만 돈이 없어요. = I like travel, but I have no money.
알지만 못 해요. = I know, but I can't do it.
notes:
지만 : connects two contrasting clauses
Attaches to verb/adjective stem: 좋지만, 가지만, 없지만
Softer contrast than 그런데; both clauses share the same subject usually

examples: 날씨가 좋은데 나가요. = The weather is nice, so let's go out.
피곤한데 일해요. = I'm tired, but I'm working.
notes:
는데/은데 : provides background context or mild contrast
는데 after verb/adjective present stem | 은데 after past/adjective consonant stem
은데 ← 좋은데, 먹었는데 | 는데 ← 가는데, 먹는데
Also used as a trailing sentence to invite a response

english: (ending) background / contrast — past or adjective context
examples: 맛있었는데 다 먹었어요. = It was delicious, but I ate it all.
작은데 예뻐요. = It's small, but pretty.
notes:
은데/는데 : mild contrast or background framing
은데 after adjective consonant stems (작은데, 없는데) or past tense
는데 after present verb stems (가는데, 먹는데)

english: (ending) intending to / in order to (after vowel or ㄹ stem)
examples: 여행을 가려고 해요. = I'm planning to travel.
이야기해 보려고 해요. = I'm trying to talk about it.
notes:
려고/으려고 : expresses intention or purpose
려고 after vowel or ㄹ | 으려고 after consonant
가려고, 살려고, 알려고 | 먹으려고, 읽으려고
~려고 하다 : be about to / planning to

english: (ending) intending to / in order to (after consonant stem)
examples: 밥을 먹으려고 나갔어요. = I went out intending to eat.
공부하으려고 → 공부하려고. = Intending to study.
notes:
려고/으려고 : intention
으려고 after consonant stems (먹으려고, 읽으려고)
려고 after vowel or ㄹ stems (가려고, 살려고)

english: (ending) I notice / I see that / how ~ — discovery or mild surprise
examples: 날씨가 좋네요. = Oh, the weather is nice (I notice).
잘 하시네요. = Wow, you're doing well.
notes:
네요 : expresses the speaker's fresh realization or observation
Adds a nuance of 'I just noticed' or 'how ~'
아/어요 states a fact | 네요 reacts to noticing it
좋네요 (I see it's good) ≠ 좋아요 (it's good)

english: (ending) as you know / isn't it / don't you think — shared knowledge
examples: 여행은 좋잖아요. = Travel is good, you know.
우리는 이미 알잖아요. = We already know, right?
notes:
잖아요 : reminds listener of something both parties should know
Implies 'you know this already' — can sound slightly assertive
Casual: 잖아 | 잖아요 is polite but still informal

english: (ending) you see / the thing is / that's because — explaining background
examples: 그게 좋거든요. = You see, that's good.
여행을 좋아하거든요. = The thing is, I like travel.
notes:
거든요 : offers a reason or background explanation to the listener
Implies the listener didn't know yet — explains why you do/feel something
Casual: 거든 | More formal: 거든요

english: (ending) nominalizer — turns a verb/adjective into a noun (gerund)
examples: 좋아지기 쉽잖아요. = It's easy to get better (lit. 'getting better is easy').
여행하기 좋아요. = It's good for traveling.
notes:
기 : makes a verb or adjective into a noun concept
먹기 (the act of eating) | 살기 (living) | 좋기 (being good)
기 vs 는 것: mostly interchangeable; 기 more common with 쉽다/어렵다/좋다/싫다

english: (ending) adverbially / so that / in a way that
examples: 완전히 다르게 보게 돼요. = You come to see it completely differently.
새롭게 느껴져요. = It feels fresh/new.
notes:
게 : converts an adjective to an adverb (크다→크게 = largely/greatly)
Also in ~게 되다 : come to do/become (gradually): 알게 됐어요 = I came to know
~게 하다 : make/let someone do: 웃게 해요 = makes me laugh

english: (ending) polite present tense (bright vowel stem: ㅏ ㅗ)
examples: 가요. = I go / going.
좋아요. = It's good / I like it.
notes:
아요/어요 : polite informal present tense (해요체)
아요 after ㅏ or ㅗ stem: 가요 (가다), 봐요 (보다→봐요), 좋아요 (좋다)
어요 after all other stems: 먹어요, 없어요, 해요 (하다)
해요 from 하다 is a special case

examples: 먹어요. = I eat.
없어요. = There isn't / I don't have.
notes:
아요/어요 : polite informal present (해요체)
어요 after stems without ㅏ/ㅗ: 먹어요, 없어요, 줘요 (주다→줘요)
해요 ← 하다 (special); 봐요 ← 보다 (contracts: 보아요→봐요)
This ending is the most-used polite speech level in everyday Korean

examples: 오늘도 걸어요. = We walk again today.
저도 좋아해요. = I like it too.
notes:
도 : additive particle — attaches directly to a noun, replacing 은/는/이/가/을/를
오늘도 : today too | 저도 : me too | 나도 : me too (casual)
아무도 + negative = nobody at all

examples: 저는 학생이에요. = I am a student.
오늘은 날씨가 좋아요. = As for today, the weather is nice.
notes:
은/는 : marks the topic of the sentence (what we're talking about)
은 after consonant (밥은, 책은) | 는 after vowel (나는, 오늘은)
Topic ≠ subject: 저는 often signals contrast or sets the scene

examples: 나는 학생이에요. = I am a student.
여행은 좋아요. = Travel is good (as a topic).
notes:
은/는 : marks the topic of the sentence
는 after vowel (나는, 여행은) | 은 after consonant (밥은, 학생은)
Also used for contrast: 저는 모르지만… = As for me, I don't know but…

examples: 학생이 왔어요. = The student came.
책이 있어요. = There is a book.
notes:
이/가 : marks the grammatical subject of the verb
이 after consonant (학생이, 책이) | 가 after vowel (나가, 여기가)
Unlike 은/는, 이/가 introduces new info or emphasizes identity

examples: 나가 아니에요. = It's not me.
여기가 좋아요. = This place is nice.
notes:
이/가 : marks the grammatical subject
가 after vowel (나가, 여기가) | 이 after consonant (학생이, 책이)
저가 is rare — 제가 is the correct humble form of 가
trap: 제가 (not 저가) is correct formal subject form

examples: 밥을 먹어요. = I eat rice.
한국어를 배워요. = I learn Korean.
notes:
을/를 : marks the direct object of the verb
을 after consonant (밥을, 책을) | 를 after vowel (저를, 음악을)
Can be dropped in casual speech: 밥 먹어요 is fine too

examples: 저를 도와주세요. = Please help me.
여행을 좋아해요. = I like travel.
notes:
을/를 : marks the direct object
를 after vowel (저를, 여행을) | 을 after consonant (밥을, 음악을)
Often dropped in casual speech

examples: 저와 함께 = together with me
커피와 케이크 = coffee and cake
notes:
와/과 : connects nouns (and) or marks accompaniment (with)
와 after vowel (저와, 커피와) | 과 after consonant (친구과→친구와)
랑/이랑 are informal equivalents

examples: 친구과 갔어요. = I went with a friend.
밥과 국 = rice and soup
notes:
와/과 : connects nouns or marks accompaniment
과 after consonant (밥과, 학생과) | 와 after vowel (저와, 오빠와)
In speech, 와 is often used regardless of preceding sound

english: (particle) at / in / to / on (location or time)
examples: 학교에 가요. = I go to school.
세시에 만나요. = Let's meet at 3 o'clock.
notes:
에 : static location (있다/없다), direction (가다/오다), or time point
에 vs 에서: 에 = where you are/going TO | 에서 = where the action happens
학교에 있어요 (at school) vs 학교에서 공부해요 (study AT school)

english: (particle) at / in / from (location of action or starting point)
examples: 카페에서 공부해요. = I study at the café.
서울에서 왔어요. = I came from Seoul.
notes:
에서 : where an action takes place, or the starting point of movement
에서 vs 에: 에서 = action happens here | 에 = static location or destination
한국에서 = in Korea (action) | 한국에 = to Korea (going) / in Korea (being)

english: (particle) to / for (a person or animal) — formal/written
examples: 친구에게 줬어요. = I gave it to a friend.
선생님에게 물어봐요. = Ask the teacher.
notes:
에게 : dative particle for people/animals; formal or written style
한테 is the casual spoken equivalent
에게서 / 한테서 = from (a person): 친구에게서 받았어요 (received from a friend)

examples: 나한테 줘. = Give it to me.
친구한테 물어봐. = Ask your friend.
notes:
한테 : casual spoken equivalent of 에게
한테서 = from (a person) casual: 친구한테서 들었어요 (heard from a friend)
Use 에게 in writing or formal situations

english: (particle) direction / means / by — after vowel or ㄹ
examples: 왼쪽으로 가세요. = Go to the left.
버스로 왔어요. = I came by bus.
notes:
로/으로 : direction toward, means/tool used, or change into
로 after vowel or ㄹ (버스로, 서울로) | 으로 after other consonants (왼쪽으로, 연필로)
~이 되다 vs ~로 되다: 로 marks the resulting state

english: (particle) direction / means / by — after consonant (not ㄹ)
examples: 학교으로 → 학교로. = toward school (로 used, not 으로, after vowel)
연필으로 써요. = I write with a pencil.
notes:
로/으로 : direction, means, or resulting state
으로 after consonant (except ㄹ): 연필으로, 왼쪽으로
로 after vowel or ㄹ: 버스로, 서울로, 칼로
Memory: 으로 adds a vowel buffer before the consonant-ending word

examples: 저의 가방 = my bag
한국의 문화 = Korea's culture
notes:
의 : possessive marker, like English 's or 'of'
Pronounced 에 (e) in casual speech, not 의 (ui)
저의 → 제 in natural speech | 나의 → 내
Often dropped: 한국 문화 sounds just as natural as 한국의 문화
trap: Pronounced 에 (e) in normal speech, not 의 (ui)

examples: 저만 알아요. = Only I know.
하나만 주세요. = Please give me just one.
notes:
만 : exclusive particle — only this and nothing else
오직 is a more emphatic synonym
아무도 + negative vs 만: 아무도 없어 (nobody) | 나만 있어 (only I am here)

examples: 지금부터 시작해요. = Let's start from now.
월요일부터 금요일까지 = from Monday to Friday
notes:
부터 : marks the starting point in time or sequence
Pair with 까지 (until/to) for from–to ranges
처음부터 : from the beginning | 지금부터 : from now on

english: (particle) until / up to / to (endpoint in time or space)
examples: 서울까지 얼마나 걸려요? = How long does it take to Seoul?
열시까지 기다려요. = Wait until 10 o'clock.
notes:
까지 : marks the endpoint (time or place)
Pair with 부터: 월요일부터 금요일까지 = from Monday until Friday
조차 is stronger: 이름조차 몰라요 = I don't even know the name

examples: 나랑 같이 가요. = Let's go together with me.
커피랑 빵 = coffee and bread
notes:
랑/이랑 : informal spoken equivalent of 와/과
랑 after vowel (나랑, 커피랑) | 이랑 after consonant (친구이랑→친구랑 in speech)
Use 와/과 in formal writing

examples: 친구이랑 갔어요. = I went with a friend.
밥이랑 국 = rice and soup
notes:
이랑/랑 : informal equivalent of 와/과
이랑 after consonant (밥이랑, 친구이랑) | 랑 after vowel (나랑, 오빠랑)
In natural speech, 이랑 is often shortened to 랑 even after consonants

examples: 저는 학생이에요 = I am a student
notes:
저 : formal/humble form of 나 (casual I/me)
저와 : I and… / with me
저는 : I (topic marker)

english: Thank. you (used between friends familiar with each other)
how; how are you (the hello word used most commonly at any time）
much, far away too, so, overly
average, ordinary, usual, normal, regular, general, common

examples: 주=week
말=end

english: the end[close] of the year, year-end

examples: 기말 고사 = final exam of the semester

example: 일 층에 있어요. (It's on the first floor.)
examples: 일 분만 기다려 주세요. = Please wait just one minute.

example: 이 시에 만나요. (Let's meet at 2 o'clock.)
examples: 이 층에 있어요. = It's on the second floor.

example: 삼 월에 봄이 와요. (Spring comes in March.)
examples: 삼 월에 봄이 와요. = Spring comes in March.
사 천 원이에요. = It's 4,000 won.

example: 오 분 후에 도착해요. (I'll arrive in 5 minutes.)
examples: 오 분 후에 출발해요. = We leave in five minutes.

example: 유월에 비가 많이 와요. (It rains a lot in June.)
trap: 6월 (June) is spoken as 유월, not 육월 — the ㄱ drops before ㅇ.
examples: 유월에 장마가 시작돼요. = The rainy season starts in June.

example: 칠 월에 방학이에요. (It's vacation in July.)
examples: 칠 월에 휴가를 가요. = I go on vacation in July.

example: 팔 시에 출근해요. (I go to work at 8.)
examples: 팔 층에 살아요. = I live on the 8th floor.

example: 구 월에 학교를 시작해요. (School starts in September.)
examples: 구 월에 학교가 시작해요. = School starts in September.

trap: ㅂ batchim links forward: 십일 → [시빌], 십이 → [시비]. Also: 10월 (October) is 시월, not 십월.
examples: 시월에 단풍이 들어요. = Leaves turn red in October.

english: a lot of; quite a little; thick

examples: 사랑해 = I love you (intimate)
사랑해요 = I love you (polite)
사랑합니다 = I love you (formal)

trap: Drops the 아 before a counter: 하나 개 ✗ → 한 개 ✓ (한 명, 한 시간).
examples: 사과가 하나 있어요. = There is one apple.

trap: Drops ㄹ before a counter: 둘 개 ✗ → 두 개 ✓ (두 명, 두 시).
examples: 커피 두 잔 주세요. = Two coffees, please.

trap: Drops ㅅ before a counter: 셋 개 ✗ → 세 개 ✓ (세 명, 세 시).
examples: 친구가 세 명이에요. = I have three friends.

trap: Drops ㅅ before a counter: 넷 개 ✗ → 네 개 ✓ (네 명, 네 시).
examples: 네 시에 만나요. = Let's meet at 4 o'clock.
다섯 시에 일어나요. = I wake up at 5 o'clock.
여섯 살이에요. = I am 6 years old.
일곱 시에 저녁을 먹어요 = I have dinner at 7.

trap: Double batchim ㄼ — sounds like [여덜] before consonants.
examples: 여덟 시간 잤어요. = I slept for 8 hours.
아홉 살에 학교에 갔어요. = I started school at age 9.
열 시에 자요. = I go to sleep at 10.

notes:
사랑해 : intimate relationship
사랑합니다 : very formal

examples: 학교에서 공부해요 = study at school
카페에서 만나요 = meet at the coffee shop

notes:
보내요 : present polite form
보냈습니다 : past tense formal
examples: 오늘은 집에서 시간을 보내요. = I spend time at home today.
오늘 저는 스타벅스에서 두 시간을 보냈습니다. = Today I spent two hours at Starbucks

english: work;  as in 일하다 : "to have a job" or "to work"

examples: 뭐 해요? = What are you doing?
뭐 먹어요? = What are you eating?
뭐 좋아해요? = What do you like?
notes: 뭐 : a compact form, used very often in daily conversation.

examples: 어디 가? = Where are you going?
notes:
가다: dictionary form
가: casual
갑니다: formal

examples: 어디 있어요? = Where are you?
있어요 = is/are, exists.
notes: 어디 있어요? : Depending on context, it can mean: Where is my phone? Where are you? Where is the restaurant?

english: No (a polite 'no'); Note that it's not a verb.
examples: 아니요.　학생이 아니다.　＝ He is not a student.
notes:
아닙니다 : formal
아니다 : verb, as in he "is not" a student.

examples: 집에 와요. = I come home.
notes:
오다 : dictionary form come)
에서 : at / in (where an action happens).

english: '설레다' (verb) to feel excited, heart to flutter
examples: 여행 이야기만 나오면 괜히 마음이 설레고 그래요. = Whenever travel comes up, my heart just flutters for no reason.
notes:
설레고 : connective form of 설레다 (and/so excited…)
설레다 : describes a fluttery, anticipatory excitement; not interchangeable with 흥분하다

examples: 휴가 일수가 짧은 한국 직장인들에게 여행이 인기가 많아요. = Short trips are very popular among Korean office workers who have few vacation days.
notes:
직장인들에게 : 직장인 (worker) + 들 (plural marker) + 에게 (to/for, dative)
직장 : workplace; 인 : person

examples: 엄밀히 말하면 여행이 우리 삶에 꼭 필요한 건 아니에요. = Strictly speaking, travel isn't strictly necessary in our lives.
notes:
엄밀히 말하면 : set phrase, literally 'if you speak precisely'
엄밀히 : adverb of 엄밀하다; 말하면 : if (one) speaks

examples: 여행은 우리에게 새로운 시선을 준다고 생각해요. = I think travel gives us a new perspective.
notes:
시선 : literally 'line of sight'; used figuratively for one's worldview or perspective
새로운 시선 : a fresh perspective

examples: 세상을 보는 눈이 좁아지기 쉽잖아요. = It's easy for your view of the world to narrow.
notes:
좁아지기 : 좁다 (narrow) + 아지다 (to become) + 기 (nominalizer)
좁아지기 쉽다 : easy to become narrow; opposite: 넓어지다 (to broaden)

english: '깨닫다' (verb) to realize, to come to understand
examples: 세상에는 다양한 삶이 있구나 하고 깨닫게 돼요. = You come to realize there are so many diverse ways of living in this world.
notes:
깨닫게 돼요 : 깨닫다 + 게 되다 (to come to do; gradual realization)
깨닫다 : a deeper realization than 알다; the moment something clicks

english: '고민' (noun) worry, dilemma, anguish over a decision
examples: 내 고민이 생각보다 작게 느껴지기도 해요. = My worries start to feel smaller than I thought.
notes:
고민 : deeper than 걱정 (general anxiety); implies agonizing over a specific problem
고민하다 : to agonize, to wrestle with a problem

examples: 여행은 우리에게 지금 이 순간을 선물해 줘요. = Travel gives us the gift of the present moment.
notes:
선물해 줘요 : 선물하다 (to gift) + 아/어 줘요 (does for someone's benefit)
선물 alone = gift (noun)

examples: 과거를 후회하거나 미래를 걱정하면서 많은 시간을 보내잖아요. = We spend a lot of time regretting the past or worrying about the future.
notes:
후회하거나 : 후회하다 + 거나 (or; connects alternatives)
후회 (noun) = regret; 후회 없이 = without regret

examples: 익숙한 곳을 떠나서 낯선 곳에 가면 내가 어떤 사람인지 더 잘 보여요. = When you leave familiar places and go somewhere unfamiliar, you see more clearly what kind of person you are.
notes:
익숙한 : attributive form of 익숙하다
익숙해지다 : to get used to something; opposite: 낯설다 (unfamiliar)

examples: 낯선 곳에서는 작은 것 하나하나가 새롭고 신기해요. = In unfamiliar places, every little thing feels new and wonderful.
notes:
낯선 : attributive form of 낯설다
낯 = face/stranger; 설다 = to be unfamiliar; pair with 익숙한 (familiar)

english: '들여다보다' (verb) to peer into, to look inside
examples: 여행은 밖을 보러 가는 거지만 사실은 내 안을 들여다보는 시간이 돼요. = While travel is about going out to see the world, it also becomes a time to look inward.
notes:
들여다보는 : 들이- (inward) + 여다 + 보다 (see); means to look closely into something
내 안을 들여다보다 : to look inside oneself

examples: 일정을 너무 빡빡하게 안 짜려고 해요. = I try not to pack my schedule too tightly.
notes:
빡빡하게 : adverbial form of 빡빡하다; colloquial/informal
일정을 빡빡하게 짜다 : to cram the schedule; also used of strict rules or demanding people

examples: 이제는 일부러 여유를 둬요. = Now I intentionally leave room for leisure.
notes:
여유를 두다 : to leave breathing room
여유롭다 : to be relaxed, at ease; 여유가 없다 : to have no breathing room

examples: 아주 사소한 순간들이 오래 남더라고요. = The tiny, trivial moments last the longest.
notes:
사소한 : attributive form of 사소하다
사소한 것 : a trivial thing; 사소한 순간 : a tiny moment; often used to mean 'seemingly unimportant'

examples: 길을 잃었다가 우연히 들어간 작은 골목이 기억에 남아요. = A small alley I wandered into by chance after getting lost stays in my memory.
notes:
우연히 : adverb from 우연 (chance, coincidence)
우연히 만나다 : to meet by chance; opposite: 일부러 (intentionally, on purpose)

examples: 여행을 다녀오면 제 일상을 조금 다르게 보게 돼요. = After traveling, I come to see my everyday life a little differently.
notes:
일상 : 日(day) + 常(ordinary/always)
일상적 : routine, everyday (adjective); 일상에서 벗어나다 : to escape from daily routine

examples: 유명한 관광지나 멋진 풍경도 기억에 남아요. = Famous tourist attractions and great views do stay with me.
notes:
관광지 : 관광 (sightseeing/tourism) + 지 (place)
관광하다 : to go sightseeing; 관광객 : tourist

examples: 여행을 갈 때 일정을 너무 빡빡하게 안 짜려고 해요. = When I travel, I try not to pack my itinerary too tightly.
notes:
일정을 짜다 : to make a schedule/itinerary
일정을 조정하다 : to adjust schedule; 일정이 바쁘다 : to have a busy schedule

examples: 세상에는 정말 다양한 삶이 있구나 하고 깨닫게 돼요. = You realize there are truly so many diverse ways of living in this world.
notes:
다양한 : attributive form of 다양하다
다양성 : diversity; 다양하게 : in various ways

여행을 가다 : to go on a trip
여행하다 : to travel (verb form)
해외여행 : overseas travel; 국내여행 : domestic travel
examples: 여행 이야기만 나오면 괜히 마음이 설레고 그래요. = Whenever travel comes up, my heart just flutters.

notes:
여러분 : polite/broadcast way to address an audience
more formal than 모두 (everyone)
examples: 여러분, 여행 좋아하세요? = Everyone, do you like traveling?

notes:
함께 ≈ 같이 (together); 함께 is slightly more literary
저와 함께 : together with me
examples: 오늘도 저와 함께 걸을 준비 되셨나요? = Are you ready to walk with me again today?

notes:
준비가 됐어요 : I am ready
준비하다 : to prepare; 준비 중 : in preparation
examples: 걸을 준비 되셨나요? = Are you ready to walk?

notes:
기분이 좋다 : to be in a good mood
기분이 나쁘다 : to be in a bad mood; 기분 전환 : change of mood
examples: 기분이 좋아지는 주제로 이야기해 보려고 해요. = I want to talk about a topic that makes me feel good.

notes:
주제를 정하다 : to decide on a topic
오늘의 주제 : today's topic
examples: 기분이 좋아지는 주제로 이야기해 보려고 해요. = I want to talk about a topic that makes me feel good.

notes:
이야기하다 : to talk, to tell a story
이야기를 나누다 : to have a conversation
examples: 여행 이야기만 나오면 설레고 그래요. = Whenever travel talk comes up, my heart flutters.

notes:
마음이 편하다 : to feel at ease
마음에 남다 : to stay in one's heart; 마음에 들다 : to like something
examples: 마음이 설레고 그래요. = My heart just flutters.

notes:
세상에는 : in this world
세상 = more colloquial/emotional than 세계 (세계 = formal/geographic world)
examples: 세상을 보는 눈이 좁아지기 쉽잖아요. = It's easy for your view of the world to narrow.

notes:
삶 is the noun form of 살다 (to live)
삶의 질 : quality of life; 일상적인 삶 : everyday life
examples: 똑같은 삶을 살다 보면 세상을 보는 눈이 좁아져요. = Living the same life day after day narrows your worldview.

notes:
사람들 : people (plural with 들)
사람마다 : each person; 어떤 사람 : what kind of person
examples: 한국 사람들은 여행을 정말 좋아하는 것 같아요. = Koreans seem to really love traveling.

notes:
나라 = colloquial; 국가 = formal
어느 나라 : which country; 우리나라 : our country (Korea)
examples: 다른 나라의 경우는 잘 모르겠는데. = I'm not sure about other countries.

notes:
특히 emphasizes the following item above all others
특별히 : specially (more formal)
examples: 특히 휴가 일수가 짧은 한국 직장인들에게 여행이 인기가 많아요. = Especially for Korean office workers with few vacation days, trips are very popular.

notes:
휴가를 가다 : to go on vacation
유급 휴가 : paid vacation; 휴가 중 : on vacation
examples: 휴가 일수가 짧은 직장인들에게 여행이 인기가 많아요. = Short trips are popular among workers with few vacation days.

notes:
인기가 많다 : to be very popular
인기 있다 / 인기 없다 : popular / unpopular; 인기 스타 : popular star
examples: 짧게라도 다녀오는 여행이 정말 인기가 많아요. = Even short trips are very popular.

notes:
이 순간 : this moment
그 순간 : that moment; 순간적으로 : momentarily
examples: 여행은 우리에게 지금 이 순간을 선물해 줘요. = Travel gives us the gift of this present moment.

notes:
과거 ↔ 현재 (present) ↔ 미래 (future)
과거에 : in the past
examples: 과거를 후회하거나 미래를 걱정하면서 많은 시간을 보내잖아요. = We spend a lot of time regretting the past or worrying about the future.

notes:
미래 ↔ 현재 (present) ↔ 과거 (past)
미래에 : in the future; 미래를 꿈꾸다 : to dream of the future
examples: 미래를 걱정하면서 많은 시간을 보내잖아요. = We spend a lot of time worrying about the future.

notes:
걱정하다 : to worry
걱정 마세요 : don't worry; 걱정이 많다 : to have many worries
examples: 미래를 걱정하면서 많은 시간을 보내잖아요. = We spend a lot of time worrying about the future.

notes:
풍경이 아름답다 : the scenery is beautiful
풍경화 : landscape painting; 도시 풍경 : city landscape
examples: 새로운 풍경을 보고, 새로운 냄새를 맡고, 새로운 소리를 들으면서요. = Seeing new scenery, smelling new smells, hearing new sounds.

notes:
냄새를 맡다 : to smell something
좋은 냄새 : nice smell; 냄새가 나다 : to give off a smell
examples: 새로운 냄새를 맡고, 새로운 소리를 들으면서요. = Smelling new smells, hearing new sounds.

notes:
소리를 내다 : to make a sound
큰 소리 : loud sound; 목소리 : voice (literally neck-sound)
examples: 새로운 소리를 들으면서 지금 이 순간에 집중하게 돼요. = Hearing new sounds, you find yourself focused on the present.

notes:
선물을 주다 / 받다 : to give / receive a gift
선물하다 : to give as a gift
examples: 여행은 우리에게 지금 이 순간을 선물해 줘요. = Travel gives us the gift of this present moment.

notes:
자신 = oneself (reflexive)
자신감 : self-confidence; 자신을 믿다 : to believe in oneself
examples: 여행은 우리 자신을 알게 해 줘요. = Travel helps us know ourselves.

notes:
내 모습 : my (true) self / my appearance
다양한 모습 : various sides; 모습을 드러내다 : to reveal oneself
english: '모습' (noun) appearance, figure, self, side (of a person)
examples: 평소에는 몰랐던 내 모습을 발견하게 되어요. = You discover sides of yourself you never knew in everyday life.

notes:
해외여행 : overseas travel
해외에서 : abroad; 해외로 나가다 : to go overseas
examples: 꼭 비행기를 타고 외국에 가는 것만 여행이 아니더라고요. = Traveling doesn't have to mean going abroad by plane.

notes:
장소를 정하다 : to decide on a place
공공 장소 : public place; 어떤 장소 : what kind of place
examples: 장소가 그렇게 중요한 건 아니더라고요. = The place isn't as important as I thought.

notes:
비행기를 타다 : to take/board a plane
비행기표 : plane ticket; 비행 : flight (noun)
examples: 꼭 비행기를 타고 외국에 가는 것만 여행이 아니더라고요. = Traveling doesn't have to mean getting on a plane and going abroad.

notes:
외국에 가다 : to go abroad
외국어 : foreign language; 외국인 : foreigner
examples: 비행기를 타고 외국에 가는 것만 여행이 아니더라고요. = Traveling doesn't have to mean going abroad by plane.

notes:
우리 동네 : our neighborhood / my neighborhood
동네 사람 : neighborhood person; 동네 산책 : neighborhood walk
examples: 우리 동네에서 평소에 안 가 본 길을 걸어 보는 것도 여행이에요. = Walking a road in your neighborhood you've never been down is also travel.

notes:
거리가 멀다 : the distance is far
거리가 가깝다 : close by; 거리 = also means street/road in context
examples: 중요한 건 거리가 아니라 새로운 걸 경험하려는 마음인 것 같아요. = What matters isn't the distance but the desire to experience something new.

notes:
경험하다 : to experience
경험이 많다 : to have a lot of experience; 경험을 쌓다 : to build experience
examples: 새로운 걸 경험하려는 마음인 것 같아요. = It's about the desire to experience something new.

notes:
기억에 남다 : to remain in memory, to be memorable
기억하다 : to remember; 기억이 안 나다 : can't remember
examples: 여행에서 가장 기억에 남는 게 뭔지 아세요? = Do you know what stays with me most from my travels?

notes:
골목길 : alley/side street (길 = road)
좁은 골목 : narrow alley; 골목 안으로 : into the alley
examples: 길을 잃었다가 우연히 들어간 작은 골목이 기억에 남아요. = A small alley I wandered into after getting lost stays in my memory.

notes:
식당에 가다 : to go to a restaurant
식당을 고르다 : to choose a restaurant; 학교 식당 : school cafeteria
examples: 이름도 모르는 식당에서 먹은 맛있는 음식이 기억에 남아요. = A delicious meal at a restaurant whose name I don't even know stays in my memory.

notes:
대화를 나누다 : to have a conversation
대화하다 : to converse; 짧은 대화 : brief conversation
examples: 길에서 우연히 만난 사람과 나눈 짧은 대화가 기억에 남아요. = A brief conversation with a stranger I met on the street stays in my memory.

notes:
도시 ↔ 시골 (countryside)
도시 생활 : city life; 대도시 : big city; 소도시 : small city
examples: 그러면 그 도시가 훨씬 더 깊게 마음에 남는 것 같아요. = Then the city stays much more deeply in my heart.

notes:
행복하다 : to be happy
행복을 찾다 : to look for happiness; 행복감 : sense of happiness
examples: 여행은 행복을 찾는 일이라기보다 행복을 느끼는 연습 같아요. = Travel is less about finding happiness and more like practicing feeling it.

notes:
연습하다 : to practice
연습이 필요하다 : practice is needed; 연습 문제 : practice problem
examples: 여행은 행복을 느끼는 연습 같다는 생각도 들어요. = I think travel is like practicing feeling happiness.

notes:
출근하다 : to go to work
출근길 : commute (road to work); 퇴근 : leaving work (opposite)
english: '출근' (noun/verb) commute to work; to go to work
examples: 평범한 출근길도 조금만 다르게 바라보면 새롭게 느껴질 수 있어요. = Even the ordinary commute, if you look at it just a little differently, can feel fresh.

notes:
산책하다 : to take a walk
산책길 : walking path; 강아지 산책 : walking a dog
english: '산책' (noun/verb) walk, stroll; to take a walk
examples: 늘 가던 동네 산책길도 조금만 다르게 바라보면 새로워요. = Even the usual neighborhood walk feels fresh with a slightly different perspective.

notes:
계획을 세우다 : to make a plan
계획에 없다 : unplanned; 계획대로 : as planned
examples: 계획에 없던 순간들이 오히려 제일 소중한 기억이 되더라고요. = The unplanned moments turn out to be the most precious memories.

notes:
시간을 보내다 : to spend time
시간이 있다/없다 : to have/not have time; 시간이 지나다 : time passes
examples: 과거를 후회하거나 미래를 걱정하면서 많은 시간을 보내잖아요. = We spend a lot of time regretting the past or worrying about the future.

notes:
정말 ≈ 진짜 (really); 정말 is slightly more standard/polite
정말요? : Really? (expressing surprise)
examples: 한국 사람들은 여행을 정말 좋아하는 것 같아요. = Koreans seem to really love traveling.

notes:
오히려 signals the opposite of what you might expect
Used when reality is the reverse of expectation
english: '오히려' (adverb) rather, on the contrary, if anything
examples: 아주 사소한 순간들이 오히려 오래 남더라고요. = The tiny moments actually last even longer.

notes:
솔직히 말하면 : to be honest
솔직하다 : to be honest (adjective); 솔직한 사람 : an honest person
examples: 솔직히 우리는 평소에 과거를 후회하거나 미래를 걱정하면서 많은 시간을 보내잖아요. = Honestly, in everyday life we spend a lot of time regretting the past or worrying about the future.

notes:
한참 동안 : for a long time
한참 후에 : after a long while; 한참을 기다리다 : to wait a long time
english: '한참' (adverb/noun) for a long time, for a while
examples: 여행하면서 본 것들, 느낀 것들이 한참 동안 제 마음에 남아 있거든요. = The things I saw and felt while traveling stay in my heart for a long time.

notes:
매일 = every day
매주 : every week; 매달 : every month; 매년 : every year
examples: 매일 똑같은 곳에서 똑같은 삶을 살다 보면 세상을 보는 눈이 좁아지기 쉽잖아요. = Living the same life in the same place day after day, it's easy for your worldview to narrow.

notes:
꼭 해야 해요 : you must do it
꼭 오세요 : please come for sure; 꼭 안다 : to hug tightly (different meaning!)
examples: 꼭 해외여행이어야지 라고 생각했어요. = I thought it absolutely had to be overseas travel.

notes:
새로운 : attributive form (before noun)
새로운 것 : something new; 새롭게 느끼다 : to feel fresh/anew
examples: 여행은 우리에게 새로운 시선을 준다고 생각해요. = I think travel gives us a new perspective.

notes:
완전히 = completely
완전하다 : to be complete/perfect; 완전한 : perfect, complete (adjective)
examples: 여행을 가면 완전히 다른 세상을 보게 되어요. = When you travel, you get to see a completely different world.

notes:
신기한 : attributive form (before noun)
신기하게도 : strangely enough; 신기하다 is used for pleasant surprise, not scary-strange
english: '신기하다' (adjective) fascinating, amazing, strange in a good way
examples: 낯선 곳에서는 작은 것 하나하나가 새롭고 신기하잖아요. = In unfamiliar places, every little thing feels new and fascinating.

notes:
소중한 : attributive form (before noun)
소중하게 여기다 : to cherish; 소중한 사람 : a precious/dear person
examples: 계획에 없던 순간들이 오히려 제일 소중한 기억이 되더라고요. = The unplanned moments turn out to be the most precious memories.

notes:
중요한 : attributive form (before noun)
중요성 : importance; 가장 중요한 것 : the most important thing
examples: 중요한 건 거리가 아니라 새로운 걸 경험하려는 마음인 것 같아요. = What matters isn't the distance but the desire to experience something new.

notes:
특별한 : attributive form (before noun)
특별히 : specially; 특별한 날 : a special day; ↔ 평범한 (ordinary)
examples: 잊지 못할 특별한 순간이 있었나요? = Is there a special moment from traveling that you can't forget?

notes:
떠나다 : to leave a place
고향을 떠나다 : to leave hometown; 여행을 떠나다 : to set out on a trip
english: '떠나다' (verb) to leave, to depart, to set out
examples: 여행은 떠나는 것도 좋지만 돌아와서 내 삶을 다시 사랑하게 되는 것도 참 좋아요. = Travel isn't just good for the going — coming back and loving your own life again is wonderful too.

notes:
돌아오다 = to return (coming direction)
돌아가다 = to go back (going direction); 집에 돌아오다 : to come back home
examples: 여행을 다녀오면 돌아온 후가 더 좋을 때가 있어요. = Sometimes I like the time after coming back from a trip even more.

notes:
느끼다 vs 감정 : 느끼다 is the verb "to feel"
느낌 : feeling (noun); 행복을 느끼다 : to feel happiness
examples: 여행은 행복을 느끼는 연습 같아요. = Travel is like practicing feeling happiness.

notes:
발견하다 : to discover (often something previously unknown)
발견 : discovery (noun); 새로운 것을 발견하다 : to discover something new
english: '발견하다' (verb) to discover, to find, to realize
examples: 평소에는 몰랐던 내 모습을 발견하게 되어요. = You discover sides of yourself you never knew in everyday life.

notes:
바뀌다 : passive; the subject is changed by something else
바꾸다 : active, to change something; 생각이 바뀌다 : one's mind changes
examples: 요즘 제 생각은 조금 바뀌었어요. = But lately my thinking has changed a little.

notes:
멋진 : attributive form (before noun)
멋있다 : also "cool/good-looking"; 멋진 사람 : a cool/stylish person
examples: 사실 유명한 관광지나 멋진 풍경도 기억에 남지만 오히려 아주 사소한 순간들이 오래 남더라고요. = Famous sights and great views do stay with me, but the tiny moments last even longer.

notes:
문화적 : cultural (adjective)
한국 문화 : Korean culture; 문화 차이 : cultural difference
examples: 나와 다른 음식, 다른 문화를 보면서 세상에 다양한 삶이 있구나 하고 깨닫게 돼요. = Seeing different foods and different cultures, you realize there are so many ways of living.

notes:
가깝다 ↔ 멀다 (far)
가까운 곳 : a nearby place; 가까운 사람 : someone close to you
examples: 멀든 가깝든 상관없어요. = Whether far or near, it doesn't matter.

notes:
멀다 ↔ 가깝다 (close)
멀리 : far away (adverb); 거리가 멀다 : the distance is far
examples: 멀든 가깝든 상관없어요. = Whether far or near, it doesn't matter.

notes:
기쁘다 : joyful, glad (more intense than 좋다)
기쁨 : joy (noun); 기쁜 소식 : good news
examples: 기분이 좋아지는 주제로 이야기해 보려고 해요. = I want to talk about a topic that makes me feel good.

notes:
가끔 ↔ 항상 (always), 자주 (often)
가끔씩 : from time to time; 가끔 있다 : happens sometimes
examples: 여러분도 가끔은 익숙한 곳을 떠나서 새로운 세상을 만나 보셨으면 좋겠어요. = I hope you too will sometimes leave familiar places and encounter a new world.

notes:
다시 = again
다시 한 번 : one more time; 다시 시작하다 : to start again
examples: 돌아와서 내 삶을 다시 사랑하게 되는 것도 참 좋아요. = Coming back and falling in love with your own life again is wonderful too.

notes:
오래 ↔ 잠깐 (briefly)
오래되다 : to become old/aged; 오래 기다리다 : to wait long
examples: 아주 사소한 순간들이 오래 남더라고요. = The tiny, trivial moments last the longest.

notes:
먼저 = first (in order)
먼저 하다 : to do first; 먼저 가세요 : please go first
examples: 먼저 여행은 우리에게 새로운 시선을 준다고 생각해요. = First, I think travel gives us a new perspective.

notes:
하는 · 해요 · 했어요
해요 = 하+여요 (contracted). 했어요 (past).
